wrapUp: England and Norway are into a Miami quarter-final after Jude Bellingham's quickfire double and Harry Kane's penalty carried the ten-man Three Lions past Mexico at the Azteca, while Erling Haaland's late brace dumped Brazil out in New Jersey. Spain joined them by edging Portugal through Mikel Merino's stoppage-time winner in Dallas, ending Cristiano Ronaldo's World Cup career and setting up a Los Angeles quarter-final against the USA or Belgium. Jarell Quansah's red card and Morocco's ongoing doubts over Ismael Saibari are the main fitness and selection issues before the next round, with FIFA's handling of Folarin Balogun's suspension adding disciplinary uncertainty across the bracket. Each story below links to the specific report it is drawn from; squad and fitness details reflect the latest available reporting as of 6 July 2026.

sections.heading: Injuries and fitness doubts
sections.description: Players ruled out, late replacements and day-to-day concerns that could change starting line-ups and squad depth.

stories.headline: Quansah set to miss Norway tie after Azteca dismissal
stories.summary: Jarell Quansah was sent off in the 54th minute of England's 3-2 win over Mexico after a VAR review of a high challenge on Jesus Gallardo, meaning he is suspended for the 11 July quarter-final against Norway unless FIFA intervene. BBC Sport reported the FA is considering its options after Folarin Balogun's one-match ban was suspended, deepening Thomas Tuchel's right-back crisis with Reece James still building match fitness.

stories.headline: Saibari scan leaves Morocco waiting before France tie
stories.summary: Ismael Saibari left Morocco's 3-0 win over Canada after 22 minutes with a thigh problem and Mohamed Ouahbi said the extent of the injury would become clearer in the coming days. Reports on 6 July said the midfielder was travelling with the squad to Boston for an MRI scan before the 9 July quarter-final against France, with Soufiane Rahimi the most likely replacement after scoring in Houston.

stories.headline: Paqueta missed Norway tie as Brazil's World Cup ended
stories.summary: Lucas Paqueta did not feature against Norway after limping off with a hamstring injury in Brazil's Round of 32 win over Japan, with Carlo Ancelotti starting Gabriel Martinelli in his place. AP and ESPN both reported Paqueta was not expected to recover in time, and Brazil's 2-1 defeat in East Rutherford confirmed their earliest World Cup exit since 1990 without their main midfield link player.

sections.heading: Squad selection and tactical reshuffles
sections.description: Omissions, late call-ups and manager decisions that reveal how teams plan to navigate the expanded format.

stories.headline: England's right-back options narrow before Norway
stories.summary: BBC Sport reported that Reece James has been unavailable since injuring a hamstring against Ghana and missed the Panama, DR Congo and Mexico ties, while Quansah's red card removes the defender who had started the last-16 match. Thomas Tuchel has already used James, Djed Spence, Ezri Konsa, John Stones, Quansah and Declan Rice on the right during the tournament, so the Norway selection is likely to turn on Spence or a reshaped back line.

stories.headline: Bellingham and Kane carry ten-man England past Mexico
stories.summary: Jude Bellingham scored twice in 98 seconds and Harry Kane converted a penalty as England became the first team to beat Mexico at the Estadio Azteca, winning 3-2 despite Jarell Quansah's red card. Thomas Tuchel reshuffled with John Stones, Dan Burn and Djed Spence after the dismissal, and England held on through 11 minutes of stoppage time to set up a quarter-final with Erling Haaland's Norway in Miami.

stories.headline: Merino super-sub settles Iberian derby for Spain
stories.summary: Luis de la Fuente kept an unchanged Spain side in Dallas but turned to Mikel Merino in the closing stages, and the substitute scored in the first minute of stoppage time after Ferran Torres slid a pass through for him to beat Diogo Costa. Spain advance to face the USA or Belgium in Los Angeles on 10 July, while Portugal bow out with Cristiano Ronaldo's international career likely over at 41.

stories.headline: Haaland's late double sends Norway past Brazil
stories.summary: Erling Haaland scored in the 79th and 90th minutes to turn a tight Round of 16 tie Norway's way after Orjan Nyland had saved a Bruno Guimaraes penalty in the first half. Julian Ryerson returned from a thigh injury at MetLife Stadium, Neymar's stoppage-time spot kick was only a consolation, and Norway will face England in Miami on 11 July with Haaland level on seven tournament goals with Lionel Messi.

sections.heading: Disruption and preparation issues
sections.description: Travel, visa and logistical problems that could affect match readiness rather than just headlines.

stories.headline: Balogun reprieve raises questions over Quansah ban
stories.summary: FIFA suspended Folarin Balogun's one-match ban after his red card against Bosnia and Herzegovina, allowing the USA forward to face Belgium in Seattle, and BBC Sport reported UEFA criticised the decision as a threat to the competition's integrity. Thomas Tuchel said the precedent had created confusion over whether England could pursue a similar route for Quansah, with tournament regulations still stating a red card brings an automatic suspension for the next match.

stories.headline: Spain know their quarter-final path before Seattle kicks off
stories.summary: Spain's 1-0 win over Portugal in Arlington was the first of two last-16 ties on 6 July, meaning Luis de la Fuente's side will face the USA or Belgium in Los Angeles on 10 July while the Seattle winner was still to be decided. The staggered schedule gives the co-hosts or Belgium a clear target, but also leaves less recovery time for whoever advances from the evening game in the Pacific north-west.

stories.headline: France survive heat and physical Paraguay approach
stories.summary: France's 1-0 win over Paraguay was played under an extreme heat warning with temperatures around 38C, and ESPN reported that Paraguay's defensive approach kept the match tight until Kylian Mbappe's 70th-minute penalty. AP detailed repeated flashpoints around Mbappe and post-match tension, making recovery and discipline part of France's short lead-in to Morocco in Foxborough on 9 July.

sections.heading: Analysis and tournament outlook
sections.description: Deeper previews on favourites, group dynamics and how the 48-team format changes the path to the final.

stories.headline: Haaland and Kane set up heavyweight Miami quarter-final
stories.summary: Norway's 2-1 win over Brazil and England's 3-2 Azteca survival mean Erling Haaland and Harry Kane, both on seven tournament goals, will meet in Miami on 11 July. Sky Sports noted Haaland's brace punished Brazil's wastefulness after Nyland's penalty save, while ESPN highlighted England as the first team ever to beat Mexico at the Azteca, giving the quarter-final a clear clash of Europe's two most decisive No 9s.

stories.headline: Spain grind past Portugal as Ronaldo's World Cup ends
stories.summary: Spain were not at their fluent best in Dallas, with ESPN reporting Portugal's expected goals of 0.60 against Spain's 1.77, but Merino's late intervention kept de la Fuente's side on course for a fifth straight clean sheet in the competition. Cristiano Ronaldo at 41 has likely played his last World Cup match, while Spain now carry momentum and a clearer recovery window than whoever emerges from Seattle.

stories.headline: Brazil's quarter-final streak ends in New Jersey
stories.summary: Brazil lost 2-1 to Norway despite dominating possession and generating a higher expected-goals figure, with Haaland's two late strikes punishing Bruno Guimaraes' missed penalty and several other wasted chances. FIFA's match report confirmed it was Brazil's earliest World Cup exit since 1990, ending an eight-tournament run of quarter-final appearances and opening the door for a first Scandinavian semi-final since 1998.

stories.headline: Mbappe's France meet Morocco with fitness clouds on both sides
stories.summary: France reached the quarter-finals through Mbappe's penalty against Paraguay in extreme Philadelphia heat, but Morocco arrive in Foxborough unbeaten in 34 matches and buoyed by Azzedine Ounahi's double against Canada. ESPN's Canada report and subsequent fitness updates leave Saibari as a major doubt, so Didier Deschamps may face a counter-attacking side reshaped around Ounahi and Rahimi rather than Morocco's leading tournament scorer.
